This internal conflict weakens the Seleucid Empire's ability to suppress the Maccabean Revolt and creates opportunities for Judean resistance. The conflict between Demetrius and Lysias is a major factor in the eventual Seleucid withdrawal from Judea.

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews XII.10.1

events.certainty_note: Demetrius I's challenge to the Seleucid throne is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Most scholars place it in 161 or 160 BCE. The circumstances of his escape from Rome and his return to the Seleucid Empire are debated. Some sources suggest he was aided by the Romans, while others claim he escaped on his own.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Judas's death at Elasa is uncertain; some scholars place it in 160 BCE rather than 161 BCE. The accounts in 1 and 2 Maccabees differ significantly, with 2 Maccabees providing a more legendary account that may not be historically reliable. The claim that Judas defeated a much larger Seleucid force in earlier battles is questioned by some scholars. The embassy to Rome is attested only in Jewish sources and is not independently verified by Roman sources. The exact nature and terms of the Roman-Judean alliance are unclear. Demetrius I's escape from Rome and return to the Seleucid Empire is variously reported in sources, and the extent of Roman involvement is debated.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 161 BCE remains heavily dependent on Jewish sources compiled well after the events. The accounts of Judas's death and the embassy to Rome are central to Jewish tradition but are not independently verified. The Seleucid perspective on the Maccabean Revolt and the internal conflict between Demetrius and Lysias is largely lost. Roman sources do not provide detailed accounts of the embassy or the early Roman-Judean relationship. The chronology of the Maccabean Revolt remains one of the most debated topics in ancient history, with scholars disagreeing on the dates of key events by several years. This creates significant uncertainty about the precise sequence and dating of events in 161 BCE.

graph_edges.note: The Roman alliance strengthened Judea's position after Judas's death
Judas's death led to Simon and Jonathan's leadership of the revolt
Demetrius I's challenge to the throne led to his victory and the beginning of his reign

era_context: The Maccabean Revolt is in its third year, with Judas Maccabeus consolidating military victories and gaining control of much of Judea. Antiochus IV Epiphanes dies (likely in late 163 or early 162 BCE), and his young son Antiochus V Eupator succeeds him under the regency of Lysias. The Seleucid Empire faces internal instability and external pressures. Rome continues to expand its influence in the Mediterranean.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Han records are sparse for this period)

events.title: Death of Antiochus IV Epiphanes
events.description: Antiochus IV Epiphanes dies, likely in late 163 or early 162 BCE, during or shortly after his eastern campaigns. His death is a watershed moment for the Seleucid Empire and the Maccabean Revolt. His young son Antiochus V Eupator succeeds him, but real power falls to the regent Lysias. The death of the architect of the persecution removes the primary driver of Judean resistance, though the Seleucid state continues to oppose the revolt. Antiochus IV's death marks the beginning of the end of Seleucid hegemony in the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews XII.7.2

events.certainty_note: Antiochus IV's death is well-attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Most scholars place it in late 163 or early 162 BCE. The cause of death is variously reported as illness, divine punishment (in Jewish sources), or complications from his eastern campaigns. The precise location of his death (Babylon, Ecbatana, or elsewhere) is also debated.

events.title: Judas Maccabeus Purifies and Rededicated the Second Temple
events.description: Following his military victories, Judas Maccabeus leads a force into Jerusalem and purifies the Second Temple, which had been desecrated by Antiochus IV. He removes the altar to Zeus Olympios, restores the Jewish altar, and rekindles the eternal lamp. This rededication is celebrated as the Festival of Hanukkah (Chanukah), commemorating the restoration of Jewish religious practice. The rededication is a symbolic and religious victory that consolidates Judean morale and legitimizes Judas's leadership as a defender of Jewish faith.

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews XII.7.6

events.certainty_note: The rededication of the Temple is a central event in Jewish tradition and is attested in multiple sources. However, the exact date is uncertain. 1 Maccabees dates it to the 25th of Kislev (November/December), but whether this occurred in 164, 163, or 162 BCE is debated. The eight-day festival of Hanukkah commemorates this event, but the historical accuracy of the eight-day duration and the miracle of the oil are not independently verified.

events.title: Lysias Leads Seleucid Military Campaign Against Judea
events.description: Lysias, regent for the young Antiochus V, launches a major military campaign against Judea to suppress the Maccabean Revolt. He leads a large Seleucid army, including war elephants, against Judas Maccabeus. The campaign is initially successful, but Judas's forces inflict significant casualties. The campaign demonstrates the Seleucid Empire's continued military strength but also the difficulty of suppressing a determined guerrilla force. The campaign is eventually inconclusive, and Lysias is forced to negotiate with the Judean rebels.

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews XII.7.4

events.certainty_note: Lysias's campaign is attested in multiple Jewish sources, but the exact date and details are uncertain. The accounts in 1 and 2 Maccabees differ significantly, suggesting later elaboration. Some scholars place this campaign in 163 or 162 BCE; others argue for 161 or 160 BCE. The outcome of the campaign is also debated: Jewish sources claim a Judean victory, but Seleucid sources (if they survived) might tell a different story.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Temple rededication is highly uncertain. While Jewish tradition places it on the 25th of Kislev, the year is debated. Some scholars argue for 164 BCE, others for 163 or 162 BCE. The eight-day duration of Hanukkah and the miracle of the oil are not independently verified and may be later theological elaborations. The accounts of Lysias's campaign in 1 and 2 Maccabees are contradictory in significant ways (e.g., the location of the battle, the outcome, the role of Judas). The claim that Judas defeated Lysias's much larger force is questioned by some scholars, who suggest that the Seleucid forces may have simply withdrawn due to internal Seleucid politics rather than military defeat.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 162 BCE remains dependent on Jewish sources compiled well after the events. The death of Antiochus IV is better attested in Greek and later sources, but the exact date and circumstances are still debated. The Temple rededication is central to Jewish religious tradition but is not independently verified. The accounts of Lysias's campaign are heavily colored by Jewish theological and political interests. No contemporary Seleucid administrative or military records survive. The Seleucid perspective on the Maccabean Revolt and the regency of Lysias is entirely lost. This creates a significant bias toward the Judean perspective and away from Seleucid imperial strategy.

graph_edges.note: Antiochus IV's death removed the primary driver of persecution, enabling the Temple rededication
The Temple rededication prompted Seleucid military response under Lysias
Antiochus IV's death and the succession of Antiochus V led to Lysias's regency and military campaign

era_context: The Maccabean Revolt is in its second year, with Judean guerrillas under Mattathias and his sons conducting campaigns against Seleucid forces. Antiochus IV Epiphanes remains king but is increasingly preoccupied with eastern campaigns and internal Seleucid politics. The Ptolemaic Dynasty continues to rule Egypt. Rome is consolidating hegemony in the Mediterranean.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Han records exist but are sparse for this period)

events.title: Death of Mattathias; Judas Maccabeus Assumes Leadership
events.description: Mattathias, the elderly priest who initiated the Maccabean Revolt, dies after a brief illness. On his deathbed, he designates his son Judas Maccabeus as military commander and his son Simon as political advisor. Judas assumes leadership of the revolt and begins a series of military campaigns against Seleucid forces, establishing himself as a capable guerrilla commander. This transition marks the shift from a spontaneous uprising to an organized military campaign.

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews XII.6.1

events.certainty_note: Mattathias's death and Judas's succession are well-attested in Jewish sources, but the exact year is uncertain. 1 Maccabees places this in the context of the early revolt, but precise dating relies on later chronological reconstructions. Some scholars place Mattathias's death in 166 or 165 BCE.

events.title: Judas Maccabeus Defeats Seleucid Forces at Emmaus
events.description: Judas Maccabeus leads a guerrilla force against a Seleucid army under Gorgias near Emmaus. Using superior knowledge of the terrain and tactical surprise, Judas defeats the Seleucid force, which is larger in number but less mobile. This victory is a turning point in the revolt, demonstrating that the Judean rebels can defeat professional Seleucid troops and boosting morale among the Jewish population. The victory is celebrated in Jewish tradition as evidence of divine favor.

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews XII.7.1

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple Jewish sources, but the exact date is uncertain. 1 Maccabees places it early in Judas's campaign, but whether this occurred in 163, 162, or 165 BCE is debated. The accounts in 1 and 2 Maccabees differ in details, suggesting later theological elaboration.

events.title: Antiochus IV Epiphanes Campaigns in the East
events.description: Antiochus IV, preoccupied with consolidating Seleucid power in the eastern provinces, launches campaigns against Parthian and Bactrian territories. These campaigns are costly and divert Seleucid military resources away from Judea, allowing the Maccabean Revolt to gain momentum. Antiochus's eastern ambitions reflect the Seleucid Empire's broader struggle to maintain control over its vast, fragmented territories.

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews XII.7.2

events.certainty_note: Antiochus IV's eastern campaigns are well-documented in Greek and later sources, but the precise chronology is uncertain. The campaigns likely occurred in the mid-160s BCE, but exact dating varies by source.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Mattathias's death and Judas's assumption of leadership is uncertain. Some scholars place these events in 166 or 165 BCE rather than 163 BCE. The accounts of the Battle of Emmaus in 1 and 2 Maccabees contain significant discrepancies (e.g., the size of forces, the role of Gorgias), suggesting later theological elaboration rather than precise historical reporting. Additionally, the extent to which Antiochus IV's eastern campaigns directly caused the Seleucid military weakness in Judea is debated; some scholars argue that Seleucid forces were simply unprepared for guerrilla warfare.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 163 BCE remains heavily dependent on Jewish sources compiled decades or centuries after the events. No contemporary Seleucid administrative records survive. The Seleucid perspective on the Maccabean Revolt is entirely lost. The accounts in 1 and 2 Maccabees, while valuable, are theological narratives designed to celebrate Jewish resistance and divine intervention, not objective historical records. This creates a significant bias toward the Judean perspective and away from Seleucid imperial strategy or broader Mediterranean political contexts. The chronology of the early Maccabean Revolt remains one of the most debated topics in ancient Jewish history.

graph_edges.note: Judas's assumption of leadership enabled the military victories that followed
The victory at Emmaus encouraged further Judean military campaigns
Antiochus's eastern campaigns diverted Seleucid military resources from Judea

era_context: The Seleucid Empire under Antiochus IV Epiphanes is at its height, pursuing aggressive Hellenization policies across the eastern Mediterranean and Near East. In Judea, this period marks the beginning of the Maccabean Revolt against Seleucid religious oppression. Egypt remains under Ptolemaic rule. Rome is consolidating power in the western Mediterranean following the Third Macedonian War (168 BCE).
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman contact zones
East Asia (Han Dynasty records exist but are underrepresented in Western scholarship)

events.title: Antiochus IV's Persecution of Judean Jews Intensifies
events.description: Antiochus IV Epiphanes escalates his campaign to suppress Jewish religious practice, banning circumcision, Sabbath observance, and Torah study. He desecrates the Second Temple in Jerusalem, erecting a statue of Zeus Olympios and sacrificing pigs on the altar. These actions, driven by Hellenization ideology and possibly financial desperation, directly provoke the Maccabean Revolt. The persecution is documented as a watershed moment in Jewish resistance to foreign rule.
key_figures: Mattathias of Modein (Jewish priest, initiator of revolt)

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews XII.5.4

events.certainty_note: The broad outline is well-attested in Jewish sources, though 1 and 2 Maccabees were compiled in the 2nd–1st centuries BCE, decades after events. No contemporary Seleucid documentation survives. Dating to 164 BCE is conventional; some scholars place intensification slightly earlier (167–166 BCE).

events.title: Mattathias of Modein Initiates Armed Resistance
events.description: Mattathias, an elderly Jewish priest, publicly refuses to sacrifice to Greek gods and kills a Seleucid official and a Hellenized Jew at Modein. He flees to the hills with his five sons (including Judas, Simon, and Jonathan), calling for armed resistance. This act marks the formal beginning of the Maccabean Revolt and establishes the guerrilla campaign that will define the next two decades of Judean history.

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews XII.6.1–2

events.certainty_note: The event is central to Jewish tradition and is attested in multiple later sources. However, no contemporary Seleucid or external documentation exists. The exact date is uncertain; some sources place this in 167 or 166 BCE. Assigned to 164 BCE by convention in some chronologies.

events.title: Han Dynasty Consolidation Under Emperor Xuan
events.description: Emperor Xuan of Han (r. 74–49 BCE) is in the middle of his reign, a period marked by strong centralization, military campaigns against the Xiongnu, and the expansion of Han influence along the Silk Road. This year falls within a stable period of Han governance, though specific events for 164 BCE are not detailed in surviving records. The Han Dynasty is at a peak of territorial and cultural influence.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled c. 109 BCE
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu, compiled c. 111 CE

events.certainty_note: No specific events are documented for this exact year in surviving Han records. The dating is based on the known reign of Emperor Xuan. This entry reflects the broader era context rather than a discrete event.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Mattathias's revolt and the intensification of Antiochus IV's persecution is debated. Some modern scholars (e.g., Bickerman, Goldstein) argue the persecution began in 167 or 166 BCE, not 164 BCE. The traditional Jewish chronology, based on 1 Maccabees, places key events in 164 BCE, but this is derived from later compilation and may reflect symbolic or theological rather than precise historical dating. Additionally, the extent of Antiochus IV's personal involvement versus that of his officials is unclear from surviving sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 164 BCE is heavily weighted toward Jewish sources (1 and 2 Maccabees, Josephus), all compiled well after the events they describe. No contemporary Seleucid administrative or military records survive. The Seleucid perspective on the persecution is lost. For China, the Han records are more systematic but still lack granular detail for individual years. The Western Mediterranean (Roman) perspective is absent from this year's record. This creates a significant bias toward Jewish religious and cultural narratives and away from Seleucid imperial or broader Mediterranean political contexts.

graph_edges.note: Antiochus's persecution directly provoked Mattathias's armed resistance
Mattathias's revolt continued under his sons after his death
The persecution was the catalyst for the broader Maccabean Revolt (164–160 BCE)

era_context: The Maccabean Revolt continues with Judas Maccabeus achieving further military successes. Rome's hegemony over the Mediterranean is now firmly established. The Seleucid Empire is weakening under the strain of the Judean conflict and other internal challenges. Han China under Emperor Wen remains stable and prosperous. This is a year of continued conflict in Judea and consolidation of Roman power in the Mediterranean.

events.title: Maccabean campaign: Judas Maccabeus advances toward Jerusalem
events.description: In 165 BCE, following his victories at Beth-horon and Emmaus, Judas Maccabeus continued his military campaign against Seleucid forces. The Maccabees advanced toward Jerusalem, the capital of Judea and the location of the desecrated Temple. The campaign was marked by continued military successes and the growing support of the Jewish population. Seleucid forces under Lysias were forced to retreat and regroup. The Maccabean advance toward Jerusalem set the stage for the eventual rededication of the Temple in 164 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The Maccabean advance toward Jerusalem is attested in Jewish sources. The exact dating and sequence of events are somewhat uncertain due to the later compilation of the sources and variations between different accounts.

events.title: Antiochus IV's eastern campaign and death
events.description: In 165 BCE (or possibly 164 BCE, dating is disputed), Antiochus IV embarked on a military campaign to the eastern parts of the Seleucid Empire, possibly to Parthian territories or to suppress rebellions. This campaign diverted Seleucid military resources away from Judea and weakened the Seleucid response to the Maccabean Revolt. Antiochus IV died during or shortly after this campaign, in 164 BCE. His death marked a turning point in the Seleucid Empire, as his successor, Antiochus V Eupator, was a minor and the empire was ruled by regents. The death of Antiochus IV removed the primary driver of the persecution of Judea, though the conflict continued.
events.certainty_note: The eastern campaign and death of Antiochus IV are attested in multiple sources, but the exact dating (165 vs. 164 BCE) is disputed. The location and circumstances of his death are also somewhat unclear. Polybius provides some contemporary information, but the Jewish sources are more detailed and were written centuries later.
cross_references: Follows from -168-001 (Antiochus IV's persecution of Judea)

events.title: Han Dynasty: Continued prosperity and administrative development
events.description: Emperor Wen of Han continued his reign, characterized by economic development, administrative consolidation, and relative peace. The Han Dynasty during this period was recovering from the civil conflicts of the early Han period and establishing the foundations for the later expansion under Emperor Wu. The reign of Emperor Wen is remembered as a period of stability and the beginning of Han prosperity.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The reign of Emperor Wen is well-documented in Chinese historical sources. The general characteristics of his rule are well-established.

events.title: Roman Senate: Continued oversight of Mediterranean affairs
events.description: The Roman Senate continued to exercise hegemonic oversight over Mediterranean affairs, managing relationships with Hellenistic kingdoms and ensuring that no single power could challenge Roman dominance. This period saw the consolidation of the Roman hegemonic system that would persist for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The general context of Roman hegemonic oversight is well-established. Specific events in 165 BCE are less clearly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating of Antiochus IV's death is disputed. Some sources place it in 164 BCE, others in 165 BCE or even 163 BCE. The location and circumstances of his death are also unclear; some sources suggest he died in Parthian territory, others in Seleucid territory. The relationship between his eastern campaign and his death is not entirely clear from the sources. Additionally, the extent to which Antiochus IV's death was a direct cause of the eventual Maccabean success is debated; some scholars argue that the Maccabees would have succeeded regardless due to the strength of Jewish resistance and the overextension of Seleucid resources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 165 BCE is dominated by Jewish sources for Judean events and Greek/Roman sources for Mediterranean affairs. The Jewish sources (Maccabees, Josephus) were written centuries after the events and contain theological and legendary elements. Polybius provides some contemporary information on Antiochus IV's eastern campaign, but details are sparse. Chinese sources are sparse for this year. The bias toward elite political and military history is pronounced; the experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite populations are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: The Maccabean campaign is part of the broader Maccabean Revolt
Antiochus IV's eastern campaign was partly a consequence of his aggressive policies and the drain of the Judean conflict
The Maccabean advance toward Jerusalem led to the rededication of the Temple in 164 BCE
Antiochus IV's death removed the primary driver of persecution and allowed the Maccabees to rededicate the Temple

era_context: The Maccabean Revolt is in full swing in Judea, with Judas Maccabeus leading Jewish forces against Seleucid armies. Rome continues to consolidate its hegemony over the Mediterranean following the Battle of Pydna. The Seleucid Empire is weakening under Antiochus IV's aggressive policies and the drain of the Judean conflict. Han China under Emperor Wen remains stable. This is a year of active conflict and political consolidation across multiple regions.

events.title: Maccabean victories: Battle of Beth-horon and Emmaus
events.description: In 166 BCE (or possibly 165 BCE, dating is uncertain), Judas Maccabeus achieved significant military victories against Seleucid forces. The most notable were the battles at Beth-horon and Emmaus, where the Maccabean guerrilla forces defeated larger Seleucid armies. These victories were crucial in establishing the credibility of the Jewish resistance and demonstrating that the Seleucid military was not invincible. The victories boosted Jewish morale and attracted more recruits to the Maccabean cause. The Seleucid general Lysias, commanding forces in the region, was forced to retreat and regroup.
events.certainty_note: The battles are attested in Jewish sources, but the exact dating (166 vs. 165 BCE) is uncertain. The sources were written centuries after the events and contain legendary elements, but the core facts of Maccabean military success are accepted by modern scholarship.

events.title: Death of Mattathias of Modein
events.description: Mattathias of Modein, the elderly Jewish priest who initiated the Maccabean Revolt, died in 166 BCE (or possibly 165 BCE). Before his death, Mattathias designated his son Judas Maccabeus as the military leader of the revolt. The transition of leadership from Mattathias to Judas marked a shift from the initial priestly resistance to a more organized military campaign. Judas proved to be an exceptionally capable military commander and became the face of the Jewish resistance.
events.certainty_note: The death of Mattathias is attested in Jewish sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Sources place it between 167 and 165 BCE. The designation of Judas as successor is confirmed in multiple sources.

events.title: Antiochus IV's continued military campaigns and administrative consolidation
events.description: Antiochus IV continued his aggressive policies across the Seleucid Empire, attempting to consolidate control and suppress resistance. In addition to the ongoing conflict in Judea, Antiochus was dealing with challenges in other parts of the empire. His reign was marked by military ambition but also by the overextension of Seleucid resources. The Judean revolt was a significant drain on Seleucid military capacity.
events.certainty_note: The general context of Antiochus IV's aggressive policies is well-established. Specific events in 166 BCE are less clearly documented.
cross_references: Part of -168-001 (Antiochus IV's persecution of Judea)

events.title: Roman consolidation of hegemony: Diplomatic missions and treaties
events.description: Rome continued to consolidate its hegemonic position in the Mediterranean through diplomatic missions and the establishment of treaties with various Hellenistic kingdoms. These efforts were designed to create a stable system of Roman dominance in which Hellenistic states operated under Roman oversight. The diplomatic framework established in this period would persist for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The general context of Roman diplomatic consolidation is well-established. Specific events in 166 BCE are less clearly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating of Mattathias's death and the early Maccabean battles is disputed among scholars. Some place these events in 165 BCE rather than 166 BCE. The sources (1 and 2 Maccabees) provide different accounts of the sequence of events and the roles of various commanders, suggesting that the historical record is not entirely clear. Additionally, some modern scholars argue that the Maccabean sources exaggerate the scale of early victories to enhance the narrative of Jewish heroism.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 166 BCE is dominated by Jewish sources for Judean events and Greek/Roman sources for Mediterranean affairs. The Jewish sources (Maccabees, Josephus) were written centuries after the events and contain theological and legendary elements. The perspective is heavily weighted toward the Jewish resistance narrative; Seleucid accounts of the conflict are largely lost. Chinese sources are sparse for this year. The bias toward elite political and military history is pronounced.

graph_edges.note: The Maccabean battles are part of the broader Maccabean Revolt
Mattathias's death occurred during the Maccabean Revolt
Maccabean military victories led to the rededication of the Temple in 164 BCE

era_context: The year following Rome's decisive victory at Pydna, marking the consolidation of Roman hegemony over the Mediterranean. The Seleucid Empire is weakening under Antiochus IV's aggressive but ultimately unsustainable policies. Judea is in open revolt against Seleucid rule. Han China under Emperor Wen continues its reign. This is a pivotal year for the transition from Hellenistic to Roman political dominance and for the emergence of Jewish independence movements.

events.title: Maccabean Revolt: Mattathias and Judas Maccabeus lead Jewish resistance
events.description: In response to Antiochus IV's persecution and desecration of the Temple, Mattathias of Modein, an elderly Jewish priest, initiated armed resistance in 167 BCE. After Mattathias's death (likely in 166 BCE), his son Judas Maccabeus took command of the revolt. The Maccabees, a guerrilla force, defeated several Seleucid armies in pitched battles, including victories at Beth-horon and Emmaus. The revolt was driven by religious conviction and the desire for Jewish autonomy. By 164 BCE, the Maccabees had recaptured Jerusalem and rededicated the Temple, an event commemorated in the Jewish holiday of Hanukkah. The revolt would ultimately lead to Jewish independence under the Hasmonean dynasty.
events.certainty_note: The Maccabean Revolt is well-attested in Jewish sources, though these were written centuries after the events. The broad outline is accepted by modern scholarship, but specific dates and details of individual battles are sometimes uncertain. The revolt's religious motivation is clear from the sources.

events.title: Macedonian reorganization: Division into four republics
events.description: Following the Roman victory at Pydna in 168 BCE, the Roman Senate decided to reorganize Macedon to prevent the re-emergence of a unified Macedonian state that might challenge Rome. In 167 BCE, Macedon was divided into four separate republics, each with its own government and prohibited from intermarriage or trade with the others. This fragmentation was a deliberate Roman strategy to maintain hegemony. The division lasted until 148 BCE, when Macedon was formally annexed as a Roman province.
events.certainty_note: The reorganization of Macedon is well-documented in Polybius and Livy. The date and structure of the division are clearly attested.

events.title: Roman expulsion of Antiochus IV's envoys and assertion of hegemony
events.description: In 167 BCE, Rome sent a clear message to Antiochus IV by expelling his envoys and asserting its authority over Eastern Mediterranean affairs. This diplomatic action reinforced Rome's position as the supreme power in the region and signaled that Antiochus's aggressive policies would not be tolerated. The action was part of Rome's broader strategy to establish a hegemonic system in which Hellenistic kingdoms operated under Roman oversight.
events.certainty_note: The general fact of Roman assertion of hegemony over the Eastern Mediterranean in this period is well-established. Specific diplomatic incidents in 167 BCE are less clearly documented.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Continued stability under Emperor Wen
events.description: Emperor Wen of Han continued his reign, characterized by relative peace, economic development, and cautious foreign policy. The Han Dynasty during this period was consolidating its administrative systems and recovering from the civil conflicts of the early Han period. The reign of Emperor Wen (180–157 BCE) is remembered as a period of stability and the beginning of Han prosperity, though it lacked the military expansionism that would characterize the later reign of Emperor Wu.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The reign of Emperor Wen is well-documented in Chinese historical sources. The general characteristics of his rule are well-established, though specific events in 167 BCE are not clearly delineated.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating of the Maccabean Revolt's beginning is debated. Some scholars place Mattathias's initial resistance in 166 BCE rather than 167 BCE. The sequence of early battles and the role of Judas Maccabeus versus Mattathias is also somewhat unclear due to the later compilation of the sources. Additionally, some modern scholars argue that the Maccabean Revolt was as much a civil conflict within Jewish society (between Hellenized and traditional factions) as it was a resistance movement against Seleucid rule, a nuance that the later Jewish sources downplay.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 167 BCE is dominated by Mediterranean sources, particularly Greek and Roman accounts. Jewish sources provide crucial information on the Maccabean Revolt but were written centuries later and contain theological and legendary elements. Chinese sources are sparse for this year. The bias toward elite political and military history is pronounced; the experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite populations are largely absent from surviving records.

graph_edges.note: The Maccabean Revolt was triggered by Antiochus IV's persecution and desecration of the Temple
The division of Macedon was a consequence of Rome's victory at Pydna
The Maccabean Revolt led to the rededication of the Temple in 164 BCE

era_context: Continuation of the mid-2nd century BCE Hellenistic world. Rome's dominance is now unambiguous following the Fourth Syrian War. The Seleucid Empire is weakening under Antiochus IV, who is pursuing aggressive policies in the Levant. The Ptolemaic Dynasty in Egypt is destabilized. Han China under Emperor Wen continues its cautious approach to the Xiongnu frontier. This year marks a critical turning point in Jewish history with Antiochus IV's persecution.

events.title: Antiochus IV's persecution of Judea and desecration of the Second Temple
events.description: Following his humiliation in Egypt, Antiochus IV Epiphanes turned his attention to Judea, a Seleucid vassal state. In 168 BCE (or possibly 167 BCE, dating is disputed), Antiochus issued decrees forbidding Jewish religious practices, including circumcision, Sabbath observance, and the study of Torah. He desecrated the Second Temple in Jerusalem by erecting a statue of Zeus Olympios and sacrificing pigs on the altar—acts that violated Jewish law and sparked outrage. This persecution triggered the Maccabean Revolt, one of the most significant Jewish resistance movements of antiquity and a foundational event in Jewish history. The conflict would reshape Judean politics and eventually lead to Jewish independence.
events.certainty_note: The persecution and temple desecration are attested in multiple Jewish sources and referenced by Polybius. However, the exact dating (168 vs. 167 BCE) is debated among scholars. The Books of Maccabees were written centuries after the events and contain legendary elements, but the core facts are corroborated by Josephus and Polybius.

events.title: Battle of Pydna: Roman victory over Macedon
events.description: The Battle of Pydna in 168 BCE was the decisive engagement of the Third Macedonian War (171–168 BCE), fought between Rome and King Perseus of Macedon. The Roman general Lucius Aemilius Paullus defeated the Macedonian phalanx with superior Roman tactical flexibility and discipline. The victory effectively ended Macedonian independence and established Rome as the undisputed hegemon of the Mediterranean. Macedon was subsequently divided into four republics and placed under Roman supervision. This battle is a watershed moment in the transition from Hellenistic to Roman political dominance.
key_figures: Scipio Aemilianus (Roman officer, later famous general)
events.certainty_note: The battle is extensively documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. Polybius was a contemporary observer with access to eyewitness accounts. The date, location, and outcome are unambiguous.

events.title: Roman embassy to Egypt and Syria
events.description: Following the Battle of Pydna, Rome sent embassies to Egypt and Syria to consolidate its newly dominant position. These diplomatic missions reinforced Roman hegemony and established the framework for Roman oversight of Hellenistic affairs. The embassies were part of Rome's broader strategy to prevent any single power from challenging its supremacy in the Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: The embassies are attested in Polybius and Livy, though specific details vary. The general fact of Roman diplomatic consolidation after Pydna is well-established.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Antiochus IV's persecution of Judea is disputed. Some scholars place the desecration of the Temple in 167 BCE rather than 168 BCE, based on calculations from the Books of Maccabees and Josephus. The exact sequence of events (persecution → desecration → revolt) is also debated, with some sources suggesting the persecution was a response to Jewish resistance rather than its cause. Additionally, the degree to which Antiochus's actions were motivated by religious intolerance versus political control and revenue extraction remains contested among modern scholars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 168 BCE is rich for Mediterranean and Levantine history but remains dominated by Greek and Roman sources. Jewish sources (Maccabees, Josephus) provide crucial information on Judean events but were written centuries later and contain theological and legendary elements. Chinese sources continue to be sparse for this year. The bias toward elite political and military history is pronounced; economic, social, and cultural history of non-elite populations is largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Pydna is the culmination of the Third Macedonian War
Antiochus IV's persecution triggered the Maccabean Revolt
Both events occurred in 168 BCE and represent Rome's and Antiochus IV's competing strategies in the Eastern Mediterranean

era_context: The Hellenistic world in the mid-2nd century BCE, dominated by the Seleucid, Ptolemaic, and Macedonian dynasties. Rome is consolidating power in the Mediterranean following the Second Macedonian War (200–196 BCE) and the Syrian War (192–188 BCE). The Han Dynasty rules China during the reign of Emperor Wen; the Parthian Empire is consolidating control over the Iranian plateau. This is an era of dynastic competition, Roman expansion, and the gradual decline of Hellenistic political independence.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (no contemporary written records in this tradition)

events.title: Fourth Syrian War (Antiochus IV's invasion of Egypt)
events.region: Seleucid Empire, Ptolemaic Egypt, Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Antiochus IV Epiphanes of the Seleucid Empire invaded Egypt, seeking to expand Seleucid control and exploit Ptolemaic dynastic instability. The invasion was initially successful, with Antiochus capturing much of Lower Egypt and besieging Alexandria. However, Roman diplomatic intervention—particularly the famous incident where the Roman legate Popilius Laenas drew a circle around Antiochus and demanded he withdraw before stepping out of it—forced the Seleucid king to abandon his campaign. This humiliation marked a turning point in Hellenistic geopolitics, demonstrating Rome's emerging hegemony over the Eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Polybius was a contemporary observer with access to Roman sources. The invasion and Roman intervention are well-attested in multiple independent sources.

events.title: Roman censorship of Titus Quinctius Flamininus and Manius Acilius Glabrio
events.description: Titus Quinctius Flamininus and Manius Acilius Glabrio served as censors in 169 BCE, a position of significant power in the Roman Republic. The censorship involved the revision of the Senate roll and the assessment of property for taxation. This censorship is notable for its timing during a period of Roman military expansion and the consolidation of Roman hegemony in the Mediterranean.

sources.name: Broughton, Magistrates of the Roman Republic

events.certainty_note: The names and dates of Roman magistrates are well-documented in Livy and later compilations.

events.title: Han Dynasty military campaigns against the Xiongnu
events.description: During the reign of Emperor Wen of Han (r. 180–157 BCE), the Han Dynasty continued defensive and occasional offensive operations against Xiongnu incursions along the northern frontier. The period was characterized by a policy of relative restraint and the use of tributary relationships and marriage alliances (heqin) to manage Xiongnu pressure. Military campaigns were limited in scale compared to later Han expansionism under Emperor Wu.
key_figures: Xiongnu leadership (not specifically named in surviving records for this year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Specific events in 169 BCE are not clearly delineated in surviving sources. The broader context of Han-Xiongnu relations during Emperor Wen's reign is well-established, but year-specific events are uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The 'circle incident' involving Popilius Laenas is attested in Polybius and later sources, but some modern scholars debate whether the dramatic version (Antiochus being forced to step out of a circle) is historically accurate or a later embellishment. The core fact of Roman intervention forcing Antiochus to withdraw is confirmed, but the theatrical details may be apocryphal.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 169 BCE is dominated by Greco-Roman sources, particularly Polybius and Livy. Chinese sources (Shiji, Han Shu) provide information on Han Dynasty activities but are compiled centuries after the events. African, Indian, and Mesoamerican histories are largely absent from surviving written records for this year. The bias toward Mediterranean and East Asian elite political and military history is pronounced.

graph_edges.note: Antiochus IV's humiliation in Egypt led to his subsequent campaign against Judea in 168 BCE
Part of ongoing Seleucid-Ptolemaic conflict rooted in the Second Syrian War

era_context: The Third Macedonian War is in full swing, with Rome mobilizing its military resources. Antiochus IV is consolidating his control of Egypt and preparing for further expansion. The Ptolemaic Dynasty is fractured and weakened. This year represents the height of Antiochus IV's power and ambition before Roman intervention. In China, the Han Dynasty remains stable under Emperor Xuan.

events.title: Antiochus IV's continued Egyptian campaign and expansion of Seleucid control
events.description: Antiochus IV continued his military operations in Egypt, expanding Seleucid control and influence. He moved to consolidate his position as the dominant power in the region, taking advantage of the Third Macedonian War to distract Rome. By 170 BCE, Antiochus had gained significant territory in Egypt and was at the height of his power. However, his ambitions would eventually provoke Roman intervention and lead to his humiliation at the hands of the Roman legate Popilius Laenas in 168 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The continuation of Antiochus IV's Egyptian campaign in 170 BCE is attested in sources, though the exact dating and sequence of events is not always clear. Modern scholarship generally accepts that Antiochus was actively campaigning in Egypt during this period.

events.title: Third Macedonian War continues with Roman military operations
events.description: The Third Macedonian War continued with Roman forces under various commanders conducting military operations against Perseus of Macedon. The war was grinding and inconclusive, with neither side achieving a decisive victory in 170 BCE. However, Rome's superior resources and manpower were gradually wearing down Macedonian resistance. The war would culminate in the decisive Roman victory at Pydna in 168 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Third Macedonian War in 170 BCE is well-attested in multiple sources. The general course of the war is secure, though specific battles and operations in 170 BCE are not always precisely documented.

events.title: Hellenization policies and cultural tensions in the Seleucid Empire
events.description: Antiochus IV continued his aggressive Hellenization policies throughout the Seleucid Empire, promoting Greek culture, language, and religion. These policies were particularly controversial in Judea, where the Jewish population resisted the imposition of Greek customs and the suppression of Jewish religious practices. The cultural tensions created by these policies would eventually lead to the Maccabean Revolt in 167 BCE. In 170 BCE, these tensions were building but had not yet erupted into open conflict.
events.certainty_note: The Hellenization policies of Antiochus IV are well-attested in Jewish sources, though the exact timing and intensity of these policies in 170 BCE is not precisely documented. The sources suggest that tensions were building throughout the early 170s BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Antiochus IV's Hellenization policies in triggering the Maccabean Revolt is sometimes overstated. Modern scholarship suggests that the revolt was a complex response to multiple factors, including economic grievances, political instability, and religious tensions, not simply a reaction to cultural policies. Additionally, the dating of specific Hellenization measures to 170 BCE is uncertain; the sources suggest that these policies were implemented gradually over several years.
historiographic_note: 170 BCE is better documented than the preceding years, but the record is still dominated by Greco-Roman sources focused on the Third Macedonian War. Jewish sources provide important information about Antiochus IV's policies in Judea, but these sources were compiled later and may reflect the perspective of the Maccabean movement rather than contemporary views. The record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East; events in other regions are almost entirely absent. Economic and social history is poorly attested.

graph_edges.note: Antiochus IV's Egyptian campaign was checked by Roman intervention
The Third Macedonian War culminated in the Battle of Pydna
Hellenization policies and cultural tensions led to the Maccabean Revolt

era_context: The Third Macedonian War begins this year, marking a major turning point in Eastern Mediterranean history. Antiochus IV is consolidating power and preparing for his Egyptian campaigns. The Ptolemaic Dynasty is fractured. Rome is mobilizing for war against Perseus of Macedon. This year marks the beginning of the end of Hellenistic independence in the Eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: Outbreak of the Third Macedonian War
events.region: Macedon, Greece, and the Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: The Third Macedonian War began when Perseus of Macedon, seeking to restore Macedonian power and challenge Roman hegemony, attacked Roman allies in Greece. Rome responded with military force under the command of various generals. The war would last until 168 BCE and result in the decisive Roman victory at Pydna, effectively ending Macedonian independence and establishing Rome as the dominant power in the Eastern Mediterranean. This conflict marked the beginning of the end for Hellenistic political independence.
events.certainty_note: The outbreak of the Third Macedonian War in 171 BCE is well-attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Antiochus IV's first Egyptian campaign
events.description: Antiochus IV launched his first military campaign into Egypt, exploiting the internal divisions of the Ptolemaic Dynasty. He invaded Egypt under the pretext of supporting the young Ptolemy VI Philometor against his regent brother Ptolemy VIII. The campaign was initially successful, and Antiochus gained significant territory and influence in Egypt. However, the campaign would eventually be checked by Roman intervention in 168 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Antiochus IV's first Egyptian campaign is debated. Some sources place it in 170 BCE, others in 169 BCE. 171 BCE is a reasonable estimate based on the available evidence, but the exact year is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Antiochus IV's first Egyptian campaign is disputed. While 171 BCE is a plausible date, some modern scholars prefer 170 or 169 BCE. The exact sequence and timing of events in the early 170s BCE is not clearly established in surviving sources. Additionally, the significance of the Third Macedonian War in triggering Roman intervention in the East is sometimes overstated; Rome was already committed to maintaining the balance of power in the Eastern Mediterranean.
historiographic_note: 171 BCE is better documented than the preceding years, primarily because of the outbreak of the Third Macedonian War. However, the record is still dominated by Greco-Roman sources (Polybius and Livy), which focus on military and political events. The perspective of Macedon, Egypt, and the Seleucid Empire is filtered through these sources. Jewish sources (1 Maccabees) provide an alternative viewpoint on Antiochus IV's Egyptian campaigns. Events outside the Mediterranean world are almost entirely absent from the historical record.

graph_edges.note: The Third Macedonian War led to Roman dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean
Antiochus IV's Egyptian campaign was checked by Roman intervention
Both events occurred in the same year, reshaping the Eastern Mediterranean

era_context: Antiochus IV Epiphanes continues his reign, now moving toward more aggressive policies in Egypt and Judea. The Third Macedonian War (171–168 BCE) is about to begin, which will reshape the balance of power in the Eastern Mediterranean. Ptolemaic Egypt is weakened by internal conflict. Rome is preparing for major military intervention in the East. In China, the Han Dynasty remains stable.

events.title: Antiochus IV's intervention in Egyptian affairs and Ptolemaic instability
events.description: Antiochus IV began to exploit the internal divisions within the Ptolemaic Dynasty, where the young Ptolemy VI Philometor was in conflict with his regent and brother Ptolemy VIII Euergetes II. Antiochus saw an opportunity to expand Seleucid influence in Egypt and potentially gain control of the wealthy kingdom. These interventions would escalate over the next few years and eventually provoke Roman intervention.
events.certainty_note: The exact dating of Antiochus IV's early interventions in Egypt is uncertain. Modern scholars place the beginning of his Egyptian campaigns around 170–169 BCE, so 172 BCE may be too early for active intervention, though diplomatic maneuvering likely began earlier.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Antiochus IV's Egyptian interventions is problematic. Most modern scholarship places the beginning of his active military campaigns in Egypt in 170 or 169 BCE, not 172 BCE. The entry above may be anachronistic. It is possible that diplomatic maneuvering began in 172 BCE, but this is not attested in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: 172 BCE is another year with minimal specific documentation. The historical record for this period is dominated by the lead-up to the Third Macedonian War and Antiochus IV's later Egyptian campaigns. Events in 172 BCE are rarely mentioned in surviving sources. The record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East; other regions are almost entirely absent.

graph_edges.note: Early diplomatic maneuvering led to active military intervention in Egypt

era_context: Antiochus IV Epiphanes is now firmly established as Seleucid king, pursuing policies of Hellenization and military expansion. The Ptolemaic Dynasty in Egypt is experiencing internal instability. Rome continues to consolidate power in the Mediterranean. In China, the Han Dynasty is stable under Emperor Xuan. This is a period of relative peace in the Eastern Mediterranean before the outbreak of the Maccabean Revolt.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (limited Western sources for this specific year)

events.title: Antiochus IV's early military campaigns and consolidation of power
events.description: In the early years of his reign, Antiochus IV undertook military campaigns to consolidate Seleucid control and expand the empire's influence. He moved to secure the eastern provinces and strengthen the Seleucid position against Parthian encroachment. These campaigns were part of a broader strategy to restore Seleucid prestige and power after decades of decline relative to Rome and Egypt.
events.certainty_note: The specific timing and details of Antiochus IV's early campaigns are not precisely dated in surviving sources. This entry represents the general pattern of his early reign rather than a specific dated event.

disconfirming_evidence: The historical record for 173 BCE is extremely sparse. No major events are specifically dated to this year in surviving sources. The entry above represents a generalized account of Antiochus IV's early reign rather than a confirmed event for this specific year. This highlights the danger of assuming continuous historical documentation even for the Hellenistic period.
historiographic_note: 173 BCE falls in a gap in detailed historical documentation. While we know the general trajectory of Antiochus IV's reign, specific events for individual years in the early 170s BCE are rarely dated precisely. The surviving sources (Polybius, Livy, later compilations) focus on major military campaigns and political crises, not on routine administrative or military activities. The record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean world; events in Asia, Africa, and beyond are almost entirely absent from Western historical sources.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of Antiochus IV's consolidation of power begun in 174 BCE

era_context: The Hellenistic world in the mid-2nd century BCE, dominated by the Seleucid Empire in the East, Ptolemaic Egypt in the South, and rising Roman power in the West. The Parthian Empire was consolidating control in Mesopotamia and Iran. In China, the Han Dynasty under Emperor Xuan was in its early reign. This period marks the transition toward Roman hegemony in the Mediterranean.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman contact zones
Mesoamerica (no contemporary written records in Western tradition)

events.title: Antiochus IV Epiphanes becomes Seleucid King
events.description: Antiochus IV ascended to the Seleucid throne following the death of his brother Seleucus IV Philopator. He was initially held as a hostage in Rome but was released and returned to claim the kingship. His reign would be marked by aggressive Hellenization policies, military campaigns to restore Seleucid power, and ultimately the Maccabean Revolt in Judea. His accession represented a shift toward more expansionist policies in the eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: The accession of Antiochus IV is well-attested in multiple Hellenistic and later sources. Dating to 175 BCE is standard in modern scholarship, though some sources place it in 174 BCE.
cross_references: Follows from death of Seleucus IV in -175

events.title: Roman embassy and diplomatic pressure in the Eastern Mediterranean
events.region: Eastern Mediterranean (Rome, Seleucid Empire, Egypt, Greece)
events.description: Rome continued to consolidate its influence in the Eastern Mediterranean through diplomatic missions and the threat of military intervention. The Senate maintained pressure on Hellenistic powers to prevent any single power from dominating the region. This period saw increasing Roman oversight of Seleucid and Ptolemaic affairs, setting the stage for eventual Roman conquest of the East.
events.certainty_note: Roman diplomatic activity in this period is well-documented, though specific embassies in 174 BCE are not always precisely dated in surviving sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Antiochus IV's accession is debated among scholars. While 175 BCE is more commonly cited in modern scholarship, some ancient sources and modern historians place it in 174 BCE. The significance of this event in triggering the Maccabean Revolt is sometimes overstated; the revolt was a complex response to multiple Seleucid policies rather than a direct consequence of the succession itself.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 174 BCE is dominated by Hellenistic and Roman sources, with limited perspectives from the Seleucid court itself. The Polybius and Livy accounts are our primary sources, but both were written from a Greco-Roman perspective. Jewish sources (1 Maccabees) provide an alternative viewpoint but were compiled later. Chinese and Indian sources for this period are sparse in Western scholarship. The record is biased toward political and military events; economic and social history is poorly attested.

graph_edges.note: Antiochus IV's policies as king eventually triggered the Maccabean Revolt
Followed the death of Seleucus IV Philopator

era_context: The Hellenistic world in the mid-2nd century BCE, dominated by the Seleucid Empire in the East, Ptolemaic Egypt in the South, and rising Roman power in the West. The Han Dynasty ruled China during a period of relative stability and expansion. In India, the Mauryan Empire had collapsed; regional kingdoms competed for dominance. This period marks the final decades before Rome's decisive interventions in the Eastern Mediterranean.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier knowledge

events.title: Seleucid Civil War: Antiochus IV Epiphanes consolidates power
events.description: Antiochus IV Epiphanes, having seized the Seleucid throne in 175 BCE, moved to consolidate his authority against rival claimants and internal factions. His accession marked a shift toward aggressive Hellenization policies and military expansion. This year represents the beginning of his reign, which would prove transformative and controversial, particularly in his relations with Judea and Egypt.
events.certainty_note: Antiochus IV's accession in 175 BCE is well-attested in multiple Hellenistic and Jewish sources. Dating is secure.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Emperor Wen's reign continues; administrative stability
events.description: Emperor Wen of Han (r. 180–157 BCE) continued his reign of relative peace and administrative consolidation. This period saw the continuation of the 'Reign of Wen and Jing,' characterized by low taxation, reduced military expenditure, and the growth of merchant wealth. The Han court maintained diplomatic relations with neighboring states and consolidated control over the empire's vast territories.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: Emperor Wen's reign dates are well-established in Chinese historiography. This year falls securely within his documented rule.

events.title: Ptolemaic Egypt: Ptolemy VI Philometor's reign begins
events.description: Ptolemy VI Philometor ascended to the Ptolemaic throne around 180 BCE and by 175 BCE was firmly established as ruler. His reign would be marked by conflict with his brother Ptolemy VIII and intervention by Seleucid and Roman powers. In 175 BCE, Egypt remained a major Mediterranean power, though increasingly vulnerable to external pressure.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy VI's reign is attested, but exact dating of his accession varies slightly in sources (180–179 BCE range). By 175 BCE, his rule is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Antiochus IV's accession is sometimes given as 176 BCE in certain sources, though 175 BCE is the scholarly consensus. The circumstances of his seizure of power involved disputed succession claims; some sources suggest his brother Seleucus IV's death was not natural, though this remains speculative. No disconfirming evidence identified for other events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 175 BCE is moderate but heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East. Greek and Roman sources dominate; Chinese sources are reliable but less detailed about this specific year. Egyptian records are sparse. The Seleucid succession is well-documented through Polybius, who wrote within a century of these events. Jewish sources (1 Maccabees) are later compilations but preserve earlier traditions. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are essentially absent from contemporary records.

graph_edges.note: Antiochus IV's consolidation of power enabled his aggressive Egyptian campaigns five years later
Antiochus IV's Hellenization policies directly provoked the Maccabean Revolt

era_context: Continuation of the mid-Hellenistic period. Antiochus IV Epiphanes is now firmly in power in the Seleucid realm. Rome is consolidating its dominance in the Mediterranean following the Second Macedonian War (200–196 BCE). The Han Dynasty continues its period of stability under Emperor Wen. Regional powers in India, Parthia, and the Mediterranean compete for influence.

events.title: Seleucid-Egyptian tensions: Antiochus IV's early military preparations
events.description: In the years following his accession, Antiochus IV began military preparations and diplomatic maneuvering against Egypt. By 176 BCE, tensions between the Seleucid and Ptolemaic dynasties were escalating. Antiochus sought to expand Seleucid influence in the Levant and potentially reclaim territories lost to Egypt. These tensions would culminate in the Syrian Wars (170–168 BCE).
events.certainty_note: The escalation of tensions is documented, though specific events in 176 BCE are not always precisely dated. The broader conflict trajectory is clear.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Continued economic expansion and merchant activity
events.description: During Emperor Wen's reign, the Han economy continued to expand. Merchant wealth grew significantly, and trade networks extended across Central Asia. The government maintained low taxation policies, which encouraged agricultural production and commerce. By 176 BCE, the Han Dynasty was experiencing one of its most prosperous periods, setting the stage for later imperial expansion.
events.certainty_note: The economic trends of Emperor Wen's reign are well-documented in Chinese historical sources.

events.title: Parthian Kingdom: Consolidation under Mithridates I
events.description: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE) was in the early stages of his reign, which would transform Parthia from a minor kingdom into a major imperial power rivaling the Seleucids. By 176 BCE, Mithridates was consolidating control and beginning the military campaigns that would expand Parthian territory. This period marks the beginning of Parthia's rise to prominence.
key_figures: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE)
events.certainty_note: Mithridates I's exact accession date is debated (171–165 BCE range). By 176 BCE, he was likely already ruling, though the precise chronology is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: Some sources place Antiochus IV's accession in 176 BCE rather than 175 BCE, creating minor chronological ambiguity. The exact dating of Mithridates I's accession to the Parthian throne is disputed among scholars, with estimates ranging from 171 to 165 BCE. No major disconfirming evidence for other events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 176 BCE remains moderate, with the same geographic and source biases as 175 BCE. Greek and Roman sources provide the most detailed information about the Mediterranean and Near East. Chinese sources are reliable but less granular about individual years. The Parthian evidence is primarily numismatic and later literary references, making precise dating difficult. The historical record is heavily skewed toward major powers and military/political events; economic and social history is less well-documented.

graph_edges.note: Escalating tensions between Seleucids and Ptolemies culminated in the First Syrian War
Mithridates I's early consolidation enabled later Parthian expansion

era_context: The mid-2nd century BCE continues with the Hellenistic world in flux. Antiochus IV is consolidating Seleucid power. Rome is increasingly intervening in Eastern Mediterranean affairs. The Han Dynasty under Emperor Wen maintains stability. In India, the Shunga Empire (post-Mauryan) is establishing itself. Parthia is beginning its rise under Mithridates I.

events.title: Seleucid-Parthian tensions: Antiochus IV's eastern campaigns
events.description: Antiochus IV began military campaigns in the eastern reaches of the Seleucid Empire, partly to consolidate control and partly to counter Parthian expansion. These campaigns, which would continue through the late 170s and 160s BCE, represented Antiochus's attempt to maintain Seleucid dominance in Mesopotamia and Persia. The conflict with Parthia would become one of the defining struggles of his reign.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Antiochus IV's eastern campaigns is documented, but specific events in 177 BCE are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Judea under Seleucid rule: High Priest Jason's tenure
events.description: Jason, the High Priest of Jerusalem, continued his tenure during this period. Jason was known for his Hellenizing policies, which brought Greek culture and institutions to Judea. His actions, supported by Antiochus IV, would eventually provoke resistance among traditionalist Jewish communities. By 177 BCE, the tensions between Hellenization and Jewish tradition were building.
events.certainty_note: Jason's tenure as High Priest is attested, though exact dates vary in sources. He served c. 175–172 BCE, so 177 BCE may predate his appointment. This entry reflects uncertainty about the precise chronology.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Emperor Wen's administrative reforms continue
events.description: Emperor Wen of Han continued his reign of administrative consolidation and reduced military expenditure. The Han court maintained a policy of 'non-action' (wu wei) in governance, allowing local administrators considerable autonomy. This period saw the strengthening of the imperial bureaucracy and the development of Confucian administrative ideals.
events.certainty_note: Emperor Wen's reign and administrative policies are well-documented in Chinese historical sources.

events.title: Shunga Empire in India: Consolidation of power
events.description: The Shunga Empire, which had replaced the Mauryan Dynasty around 185 BCE, was consolidating its control over central and northern India. By 177 BCE, the Shunga rulers had established a stable administration and were promoting Hindu religious practices and Sanskrit culture. The empire represented a shift away from the cosmopolitan Mauryan model toward more regionally focused Hindu kingdoms.
events.certainty_note: The Shunga Empire's chronology is debated. Pushyamitra's reign dates are uncertain (185–151 BCE or 187–147 BCE). By 177 BCE, the empire was established, but precise events are not well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: Jason's appointment as High Priest is sometimes dated to 175 BCE rather than earlier, which would place him outside his tenure in 177 BCE. The exact chronology of Shunga rulers is disputed among scholars, with some sources placing Pushyamitra's reign earlier or later. No major disconfirming evidence for other events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 177 BCE is moderate but increasingly sparse for regions outside the Mediterranean and Near East. Greek and Roman sources remain primary; Chinese sources are reliable but less detailed. Indian sources are primarily later compilations (Puranas, Buddhist texts) with uncertain chronologies. Archaeological evidence from Shunga sites provides material confirmation but limited dating precision. The historical record continues to be weighted toward major political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Antiochus IV's military consolidation enabled his later aggressive campaigns
Jason's Hellenizing policies provoked the Maccabean Revolt

era_context: The mid-2nd century BCE continues. Antiochus IV Epiphanes is consolidating Seleucid power and preparing for major military campaigns. Rome is increasingly dominant in the Mediterranean. The Han Dynasty under Emperor Wen is in its final years (he would die in 157 BCE). Parthia is rising under Mithridates I. India's Shunga Empire is establishing itself.

events.title: Seleucid military preparations: Antiochus IV's strategic positioning
events.description: Antiochus IV continued military preparations and strategic positioning for his planned campaigns against Egypt and Parthia. By 178 BCE, he had consolidated internal Seleucid power and was building up military resources. These preparations would culminate in the First Syrian War (170–168 BCE) against Egypt and ongoing conflicts with Parthia.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Antiochus IV's military preparations is documented, but specific events in 178 BCE are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Emperor Wen's final years of reign
events.description: Emperor Wen of Han was in the final years of his reign (he would die in 157 BCE). By 178 BCE, he had ruled for over two decades and had established a stable, prosperous empire. His policies of low taxation and reduced military expenditure had created economic growth and relative peace. The succession was being prepared, though the exact arrangements are not fully documented.
events.certainty_note: Emperor Wen's reign dates are well-established. By 178 BCE, he was in his final years.
cross_references: Leads to -157-001 (Emperor Wen's death and succession)

events.title: Ptolemaic Egypt: Ptolemy VI Philometor's consolidation
events.description: Ptolemy VI Philometor continued to consolidate his rule over Egypt. By 178 BCE, he was firmly established as the primary ruler, though his mother Cleopatra II retained significant influence. Egypt remained a major Mediterranean power, but tensions with the Seleucid Empire were building, setting the stage for the Syrian Wars.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy VI's rule is attested, but specific events in 178 BCE are not always precisely documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The chronology of Antiochus IV's military preparations is somewhat speculative, as specific events in 178 BCE are not always precisely dated in sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 178 BCE remains moderate, with the same geographic and source biases as previous years. Greek and Roman sources dominate; Chinese sources are reliable but less detailed about individual years. The historical record continues to be weighted toward major political and military events. Economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Regions outside the Mediterranean, Near East, and China are essentially absent from contemporary records.

graph_edges.note: Antiochus IV's military preparations culminated in the First Syrian War
Emperor Wen's reign ended with his death in 157 BCE

era_context: The mid-2nd century BCE. Antiochus IV Epiphanes is consolidating Seleucid power in the eastern Mediterranean. Rome is increasingly dominant in the Mediterranean world following the Second Macedonian War. The Han Dynasty under Emperor Wen is in its final years of a prosperous reign. Parthia is rising under Mithridates I. India's Shunga Empire is consolidating power.

events.title: Antiochus IV Epiphanes: Early reign consolidation
events.description: Antiochus IV Epiphanes, having seized the Seleucid throne around 175 BCE, was in the early years of consolidating his power. By 179 BCE, he had established himself as the dominant figure in the Seleucid realm and was beginning to implement his policies of aggressive Hellenization and military expansion. His reign would prove transformative and controversial, particularly in his relations with Judea and Egypt.
events.certainty_note: Antiochus IV's reign is well-attested, but specific events in 179 BCE are not always precisely dated. The chronology of his early reign is somewhat uncertain.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Emperor Wen's reign continues; economic prosperity
events.description: Emperor Wen of Han continued his reign of economic prosperity and administrative stability. By 179 BCE, the Han Dynasty had experienced nearly a decade of growth and relative peace. The government's policy of low taxation and reduced military expenditure had encouraged agricultural production and merchant activity. The empire was at the height of its prosperity under Wen's rule.
events.certainty_note: Emperor Wen's reign and economic policies are well-documented in Chinese historical sources.

events.title: Parthian Kingdom: Mithridates I's early expansion
events.description: Mithridates I of Parthia was in the early stages of his reign and beginning military campaigns to expand Parthian territory. By 179 BCE, he was consolidating control over Mesopotamia and preparing for conflicts with the Seleucid Empire. Mithridates would eventually transform Parthia from a minor kingdom into a major imperial power.
key_figures: Mithridates I of Parthia (r. c. 171–138 BCE)
events.certainty_note: Mithridates I's exact accession date is debated (171–165 BCE range). By 179 BCE, he may have been ruling, though the chronology is uncertain.

events.title: Ptolemaic Egypt: Ptolemy VI Philometor's reign
events.description: Ptolemy VI Philometor continued his reign over Egypt. By 179 BCE, he was firmly established as the primary ruler, though his mother Cleopatra II retained significant influence. Egypt remained a major Mediterranean power, though increasingly vulnerable to external pressure from the Seleucid Empire and Rome.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy VI's rule is attested, but specific events in 179 BCE are not always precisely documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Antiochus IV's accession is sometimes given as 176 BCE rather than 175 BCE, creating minor chronological ambiguity about his status in 179 BCE. If his accession was in 176 BCE, he would not yet have been ruling in 179 BCE. The exact dating of Mithridates I's accession to the Parthian throne is disputed among scholars. No major disconfirming evidence for other events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 179 BCE is moderate but increasingly sparse for regions outside the Mediterranean and Near East. Greek and Roman sources remain primary; Chinese sources are reliable but less detailed about individual years. The historical record continues to be weighted toward major political and military events. Economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Regions outside the Mediterranean, Near East, and China are essentially absent from contemporary records. The chronology of this period is somewhat uncertain, particularly for the Seleucid succession and Parthian expansion.

graph_edges.note: Antiochus IV's consolidation of power enabled his later aggressive campaigns
Mithridates I's expansion and Antiochus IV's campaigns were concurrent conflicts

era_context: The early Hellenistic period in the Eastern Mediterranean, with the Seleucid Empire fragmenting and Rome rising as a dominant military power. In China, the Han Dynasty under Emperor Wen was consolidating power after the Rebellion of the Seven States (154 BCE). In India, the Mauryan Empire had collapsed; regional kingdoms competed for dominance. Egypt remained under Ptolemaic rule.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman contact zones
Mesoamerica (no contemporary written records in European tradition)

events.title: Seleucid-Egyptian War (Fifth Syrian War) — Antiochus III campaigns in Coele-Syria
events.description: Antiochus III of the Seleucid Empire launched a major military campaign to reclaim territories in Coele-Syria and Phoenicia from Ptolemaic Egypt. This conflict was part of the broader struggle for control of the eastern Mediterranean following the weakening of Ptolemaic power. The campaign resulted in significant territorial gains for the Seleucids and demonstrated Rome's growing interest in Eastern affairs, as Roman envoys monitored the conflict. The war lasted several years and reshaped the balance of power in the Hellenistic world.

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab Urbe Condita)

events.certainty_note: Dating of the Fifth Syrian War is debated among scholars; some place it 202–195 BCE, others earlier. The year -180 represents a conventional placement within the broader conflict period, but exact chronology is uncertain.
cross_references: Follows from broader Hellenistic power struggles after Alexander's death
Precedes Roman intervention in Eastern Mediterranean affairs

events.title: Han Dynasty — Reign of Emperor Wen consolidates central authority
events.description: Emperor Wen of Han (r. 180–157 BCE) continued his policy of centralization and administrative reform following the suppression of the Rebellion of the Seven States in 154 BCE. His reign was marked by relative peace, economic recovery, and the strengthening of bureaucratic institutions. Wen promoted Confucian scholars and reduced harsh Legalist policies, establishing a model of governance that would influence Chinese statecraft for centuries. His period saw the consolidation of Han power and the beginning of the empire's expansion.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Han Dynasty chronology is well-established through multiple Chinese historical sources and archaeological evidence. Emperor Wen's reign dates are secure.
cross_references: Part of broader Han consolidation after civil conflict
Precedes expansion under Emperor Wu (r. 141–87 BCE)

events.title: Mauryan Empire — Post-Ashoka fragmentation in Indian subcontinent
events.description: By 180 BCE, the Mauryan Empire had effectively collapsed following the death of Emperor Ashoka (c. 232 BCE). Regional kingdoms and dynasties competed for dominance across the Indian subcontinent. The Shunga Dynasty, founded by Pushyamitra Shunga after overthrowing the last Mauryan emperor Brihadratha (c. 185 BCE), was consolidating power in central India. Simultaneously, the Chola, Pandya, and Chera kingdoms dominated the south; the Satavahana Dynasty was emerging in the Deccan. This period saw the fragmentation of centralized imperial authority and the rise of regional powers.
key_figures: Regional rulers of Chola, Pandya, Chera, and Satavahana kingdoms

sources.name: Puranas (Hindu religious texts, compiled later but preserving older traditions)
Archaeological evidence from Mauryan and Shunga sites

events.certainty_note: The exact date of the Mauryan collapse and Shunga rise is debated. Traditional sources place Pushyamitra's coup c. 185 BCE, but some scholars argue for dates as late as 170 BCE. The year -180 is used here as a conventional marker within this transition period.
cross_references: Follows from Ashoka's reign and the empire's gradual decline
Precedes the rise of the Satavahana Empire

events.title: Ptolemaic Egypt — Ptolemy IV Philopator's reign and internal instability
events.description: Ptolemy IV Philopator (r. 221–204 BCE) was in the later years of his reign, a period marked by internal political instability, court intrigue, and the influence of powerful ministers such as Sosibius and Agathocles. The Ptolemaic state, though still wealthy and militarily significant, was beginning to show signs of the administrative decay that would characterize the later Ptolemaic period. The loss of territories to the Seleucids in the Fifth Syrian War further weakened Egyptian prestige. Ptolemy IV's reign saw the consolidation of Greek-Egyptian cultural synthesis but also the beginning of the dynasty's long decline.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemaic chronology is well-established through Egyptian papyri, Greek sources, and numismatic evidence.
cross_references: Part of broader Hellenistic political fragmentation
